growth_records.cite: Carere et al. Mixotrophy drives niche expansion of verrucomicrobial methanotrophs. The ISME Journal (2017). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2017.112. [PMC5649168]
growth_records.snippet: Table 1: H2 oxidation by Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1 during chemostat cultivation. O2-replete, medium H2: growth rate 9.33 mg/l/h; CH4 consumption 2.40 (mmol gCDW-1 h-1); H2 consumption 0.03 (mmol gCDW-1 h-1)
growth_records.notes: Chemostat dilution rate D=0.02 h^-1 was maintained for all six conditions in Table 1 (footnote b); at chemostat steady state (confirmed by stable OD600 per Methods) mu = D = 0.02 h^-1, so growth_rate_per_h is reported (not calculated) from the directly-set dilution rate. CH4 and H2 specific consumption rates (mmol gCDW-1 h-1) are taken directly from Table 1 for the O2-replete, medium-H2 (0.7% v/v H2 feed) row. gCDW derived via OD600 standard curve (OD600=1 -> 0.43 gCDW/L), as stated in Methods.

growth_records.cite: Carere CR et al. Growth on Formic Acid Is Dependent on Intracellular pH Homeostasis for the Thermoacidophilic Methanotroph Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1. Frontiers in Microbiology (2021). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2021.651744. [PMC8024496]
growth_records.snippet: Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1 was observed to grow methanotrophically between pH 1.0 and 6.0 (pH_opt: ~2.5; mu_max: 0.015 h-1) in batch culture experiments.
growth_records.notes: Batch methanotrophic growth (this study). mu_max=0.015 h-1 measured from exponential OD600 at pH_opt 2.5, 50C, V4 medium, 10% CH4 v/v in air + 5% CO2. td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.015=46.2 h. CH4 consumption monitored by GC-FID but no specific/volumetric CH4 uptake rate reported. Autotroph fixing CO2 via Calvin-Benson-Bassham cycle.

up.method: chemostat; D x feed conc

sec.method: terminal oxidation product; not quantified as rate

yields.Yxs: Y_CDW/substrate = 7.83 ± 0.41 g/mol methanol; Y_CDW/e- = 1.31 ± 0.07 g/mol electron

growth_records.cite: Carere CR et al. Growth on Formic Acid Is Dependent on Intracellular pH Homeostasis for the Thermoacidophilic Methanotroph Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1. Frontiers in Microbiology (2021). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2021.651744. [PMC8024496]
growth_records.snippet: Steady state biomass productivity ... CH3OH: Biomass productivity 13.18 (±0.70) mg L-1 h-1; Y_CDW/substrate 7.83 (±0.41) g mol-1; Y_CDW/e- 1.31 (0.07) g mol-1. V4 + 12.4 mM methanol, 45C, pH 2.5, D=0.0052 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat on methanol (this study, Table 1). At steady state mu=D=0.0052 h-1; td=133 h (reported). Measured biomass productivity=13.18±0.70 mg/L/h; Y_CDW/substrate=7.83±0.41 g CDW/mol methanol; Y_CDW/e-=1.31±0.07 g/mol e- (both measured). Volumetric methanol uptake derived: qS_vol = D x S_feed = 0.0052 /h x 12.4 mM = 0.0645 mmol/L/h (assumes near-complete consumption at steady state; marked is_experimental false). Specific qS not computable (steady-state biomass concentration X not tabulated). Substrate quantified by HPLC.

growth_records.cite: Carere CR et al. Growth on Formic Acid Is Dependent on Intracellular pH Homeostasis for the Thermoacidophilic Methanotroph Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1. Frontiers in Microbiology (2021). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2021.651744. [PMC8024496]
growth_records.snippet: CHOOH: Biomass productivity 4.73 (±0.45) mg L-1 h-1; Y_CDW/substrate 2.86 (±0.27) g mol-1; Y_CDW/e- 1.43 (±0.14) g mol-1. Stable growth on formic acid demonstrated in continuous chemostat (D = 0.0052 h-1, t_d = 133 h); batch formic acid gav
growth_records.notes: Chemostat on formic acid (this study, Table 1) - key finding: formate supports growth ONLY in continuous culture (batch formic-acid addition killed cells via cytosolic acidification). mu=D=0.0052 h-1; td=133 h. Measured biomass productivity=4.73±0.45 mg/L/h; Y_CDW/substrate=2.86±0.27 g CDW/mol formate; Y_CDW/e-=1.43±0.14 g/mol e- (measured; per-electron yield nearly identical to methanol). Volumetric formate uptake derived: qS_vol = D x S_feed = 0.0052 x 12.4 mM = 0.0645 mmol/L/h (assumes near-complete consumption; is_experimental false). Specific qS not computable (X not tabulated).

Biomass productivity 5.40±0.26 mg/L/h; Y_CDW/substrate 6.79±0.55 g/mol CH4; Y_CDW/e- 0.85±0.07 g/mol e- (measured values, per Carere 2017). Included for completeness and flagged as cited.

growth_records.cite: Cartin-Caballero, C. et al. Oxygen competition induces chlorosis in thermoacidophilic methanotroph-microalgae cocultures. Biotechnology for Biofuels and Bioproducts (2026). DOI:10.1186/s13068-026-02759-1. [PMC13147883]
growth_records.snippet: Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1 was maintained in chemostat culture using a 1-L bioreactor (BioFlo 110...) with a 600 mL working volume and 0.0069 h-1 dilution rate. A gas mixture (69% CO2, 1.0% CH4, 3.1% O2, balance N2...) was continuously s
growth_records.notes: Pure-culture chemostat stock of the methanotroph. At steady state mu = dilution rate D = 0.0069 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.0069 = 100.5 h. Growth_rate is CALCULATED as mu=D. Autotrophic methanotroph fixing CO2, oxidizing CH4 with O2. V4 medium composition cited from ref CR25 (Carere et al.), not stated in-text, so medium_composition_stated=false. CH4/O2 uptake given only as gas inlet/outlet fractions (no gDW-normalized rate for this stock), so rates left null. Chemostat method/medium also cited from prior work (ref CR26).

up.method: net CH4 oxidation rate over 6-day culture from headspace gas concentrations/pressures; maximal at 0.15:1 ratio (CH4 fully removed at this ratio). Attributable to the methanotroph.
Methylacidiphilum sp. RTK17.1 strain-specific O2 consumption rate

growth_records.cite: Cartin-Caballero, C. et al. Oxygen competition induces chlorosis in thermoacidophilic methanotroph-microalgae cocultures. Biotechnology for Biofuels and Bioproducts (2026). DOI:10.1186/s13068-026-02759-1. [PMC13147883]
growth_records.snippet: CH4 oxidation rates were maximal (0.124 +/- 0.001 mmolCH4 L-1 h-1) at a Methylacidiphilum:Galdieria ratio of 0.15:1... a maximum productivity of 250 (+/- 3) mgDW L-1 h-1 was obtained at an initial 0.08:1... the relatively fast O2 consumptio
growth_records.notes: Beneficial-synergy batch coculture (low methanotroph abundance, O2-limited). CH4 oxidation (=CH4 uptake by methanotroph) 0.124 +/- 0.001 mmol/L/h is volumetric (coculture basis) and attributable to Methylacidiphilum since Galdieria does not oxidize CH4. O2 consumption 2.33 +/- 0.19 mmol/gDW/h is reported as a Methylacidiphilum strain property (may derive from companion characterization). Whole-coculture max biomass productivity 250 +/- 3 mgDW/L/h at 0.08:1 ratio. Net O2 production and net C fixation rates (Fig 2) are microalga (eukaryote) contributions and excluded. Numeric per-strain growth rate mu reported only in Fig 1 bar charts (not extractable from XML). Medium composition cited from ref, not stated in-text.

up.method: net CH4 oxidation rate over 6-day culture at highest Methylacidiphilum:Galdieria ratios; residual oxidation despite chlorosis

sec.method: excreted by Galdieria (microalga) under O2 limitation; reversible on O2 replenishment

growth_records.cite: Cartin-Caballero, C. et al. Oxygen competition induces chlorosis in thermoacidophilic methanotroph-microalgae cocultures. Biotechnology for Biofuels and Bioproducts (2026). DOI:10.1186/s13068-026-02759-1. [PMC13147883]
growth_records.snippet: Even at the highest Methylacidiphilum:Galdieria ratios, some CH4 oxidation occurred (0.062 +/- 0.016 mmol CH4 L-1 h-1)... the minimum productivity of 188 (+/- 4) mgDW L-1 h-1 was obtained when [methanotroph ratio high]... ratios >= 0.23 led
growth_records.notes: High-methanotroph-ratio coculture (O2-competition regime). Net CH4 oxidation 0.062 +/- 0.016 mmol/L/h (volumetric, methanotroph-attributable). Whole-coculture minimum biomass productivity 188 +/- 4 mgDW/L/h. Coproporphyrin III excretion is a microalgal (eukaryote) response, listed for context only. Growth inhibition 66-100% vs beneficial ratios. Numeric mu in figures only.
